mnemonics.title: L I N – Linearly Independent, None
mnemonics.content: A set of vectors is linearly independent if the only linear combination that gives the zero vector uses all coefficients zero. Think: 'L'ines 'I'ndependent 'N'othing else (zero) counts.

mnemonics.title: BIG Basis
mnemonics.content: Basis = Independent Generating set. The acronym BIG helps: B for Basis, I for Independent, G for Generating (spans the space).

mnemonics.title: DIM = Max Independent
mnemonics.content: Dimension (DIM) equals the maximal size of a linearly independent set (also the size of any basis). Remember: 'DIM = Max Independent' = 'D'I mens 'I'ndependent 'M'ax.

mnemonics.title: LCCD
mnemonics.content: Linear Combinations Create Dependence: If you can write a vector as a combination of others, the set is linearly dependent. LCCD: 'Linear Combination Creates Dependent Collection'.

mnemonics.title: RREF Ready
mnemonics.content: Use Reduced Row Echelon Form (RREF) to test independence: each pivot column corresponds to an independent vector. RREF = 'Row Echelon Form Finds Independent Pivots'.

acronyms.usage: Use when checking if a set of vectors can be expressed only trivially as a linear combination equalling zero.
Used when at least one vector in the set can be written as a combination of the others (non‑trivial solution).
A set that is both linearly independent and spans the entire vector space; any vector can be uniquely expressed as a combination of basis vectors.
The number of vectors in any basis of a vector space; also the size of the largest linearly independent subset.
The set of all linear combinations of a given set of vectors; use to see if a vector lies in the space generated by the set.
Apply Gaussian elimination to a matrix; the pivot columns give a basis for the column space, helping determine independence.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine arrows in space: if no arrow lies in the same line or plane as the others, they point in truly different directions. In 2D, two independent arrows form a coordinate frame; in 3D, three independent arrows point along x, y, z axes.
Picture a rubber sheet being stretched by a set of vectors: the span is the entire region the sheet can cover when you pull it in all directions allowed by those vectors.
Think of a coordinate system: the axes (like x‑axis, y‑axis, z‑axis) are a basis – they are independent and any point can be reached by moving along these axes.
Count the number of independent directions you can move in a space: a line has dimension 1 (one direction), a plane 2 (two independent directions), our usual space 3.
If you have three arrows in a plane, one of them can be slid to lie on top of the other two; it adds no new direction – like redundant road signs that point the same way.

analogies.analogy: Think of a team of musicians: each can play a unique instrument; if one instrument's sound can be reproduced by others, the team is dependent. Independent musicians each contribute a new sound.

The span is like the set of all possible destinations you can reach using a given set of roads: each road (vector) lets you travel in a certain direction; the span covers every place you can get to by combining those roads.

A basis is like a minimal set of primary colors (red, green, blue) that can mix to produce any other color; they are independent (no color can be made by mixing the others) and span the full color space.
Dimension is the number of degrees of freedom: a car moving on a flat road has 2 (forward/back and left/right), while a drone has 3 (forward/back, left/right, up/down).
If you have a set of keys on a keyring, and one key can open all the same doors as the others, it's redundant – you can drop it and still have the same access; that's dependence.
